title: Chapter 22: The Decision
content: The eggs have gone cold. I've been pushing them around the plate for eleven minutes — I know because I've been watching the clock on the wall, the one Tom picked out because it was "understated," which is a word men like Tom use when they mean expensive-looking-but-not-tacky. The eggs are overcooked anyway. Everything in this house is a little overcooked, a little too done, like the decorator got to the kitchen the same way she got to the living room and the bedroom and me.

Tom's voice carries from the study. Clipped. Angular. The way he talks when he's trying to keep something from escalating but it's already escalated. I've heard that voice a thousand times — at campaign dinners, at fundraiser galas, at the kinds of parties where everyone smiles with their teeth and speaks in ellipses.
"—I don't care what she said. That's not the issue. The issue is—"

He came home different last night. Smaller. That's the only word for it. Tom Hargrove has always taken up space like he's paying for it by the square foot, but last night he walked through the door and the room didn't shrink around him the way it usually does. He poured himself a whiskey at nine p.m. on a Tuesday and didn't offer me one. He didn't ask about my day. He didn't do any of the small, performative kindnesses that have substitutes for love for twelve years.
Something happened at her house. At Jax's.

I know it the way I know most things about Tom — not because he tells me, but because I've spent a decade learning to read the weather of him. The storm fronts and the high-pressure systems. The way his jaw sets when he's cornered. The way he did that thing with his jaw last night, over and over, like he was chewing on something he couldn't swallow.

He's chewing on it now, on the phone, and I'm pushing cold eggs around a plate in the breakfast room of a Bel Air mansion, and somewhere underneath all of this ordinariness is the fact that my husband is afraid.

I've never seen Tom afraid before. I didn't know the shape of it on him. Now that I do, I realize it suits him poorly. He wears fear like I wear these cashmere lounging sets — expensive, chosen by someone else, and not quite the right size.
I set down my fork. The clink is too loud in the empty breakfast room, and I flinch at the sound of myself, which is — well. That's something, isn't it? Flinching at your own fork.

Tom's voice rises, drops, rises again. I catch fragments. "Recorded." "That's not—" "She can't prove—" And then, quieter, a sound I've never heard from him before: a laugh with no humor in it, a laugh that's just air leaving a body that's run out of options.

I push back from the table. The eggs stay, untouched. Twelve years of untouched eggs. Twelve years of breakfasts I didn't choose, in rooms I didn't design, beside a man I didn't — I don't finish the thought. I never finish the thought.

The hallway to the master suite is long and white and gallery-lit, the same way everything in this house is long and white and gallery-lit. Tom wanted a house that looked like a museum. He got one. He just didn't realize he'd be the exhibit.
I open the closet door.

It's vast. Walk-in doesn't cover it — this is a walk-through, a cathedral of clothing organized by color and season and occasion, every piece curated by the stylist Tom hired after the engagement, the one who looked at my Akron department store wardrobe like it was a diagnosis. "We'll build you from the ground up," she said, and she wasn't talking about outfits. She was talking about me.

I walk past the gowns first. Black tie. White tie. Fundraiser. Gala. The red one Tom picked for the Midterm victory party. The navy one his mother chose for the Christmas card shoot. The ivory silk dress I wore the night he proposed — or rather, the night he arranged for me to be proposed to, because even that had a production team.

Past the campaign outfits next. The patriotism palette: navy, cream, soft red. The "approachable but polished" look that the consultant designed. "Voters want to see her, but not too much of her," he said, while I sat in the chair and let them pin things to my body like I was a mannequin with a pulse.

Past the "appropriate" dresses. The funeral dresses. The charity luncheon dresses. The Sunday brunch dresses. The "just-because-you're-married-to-a-politician-doesn't-mean-you-can-look-like-you're-trying" dresses. Each one a uniform. Each one a costume for a role I auditioned for at eighteen and have been playing ever since.

In the back, behind the last rack — the winter coats I never wear because Los Angeles doesn't have winter, but Tom insists on "seasonal readiness" — there's a cardboard box. Moving box. U-Haul medium with a strip of packing tape still yellowing at the edges. It's been there since the move from the Sacramento house, which was there since the move from the DC apartment, which was there since the move from the condo in Akron. Every house, every city, every life — the box comes with me and I never open it and I never throw it away.
I pull it out now. The cardboard is soft at the corners, water-damaged from some move, some rain, some carelessness. I peel back the flaps.

Cedar hits me first. The closet smells like cedar — the lining Tom had installed to protect the cashmere — but this is different. This is old cedar, closet cedar from a house that doesn't exist anymore, soaked into fabric that hasn't seen the light of day in a decade. Underneath it: rubber, warm and familiar. And something else, something that smells like Akron in May, which is to say like rain on hot asphalt and cheap shampoo and the particular nothing-smell of a rooftop that no one's supposed to be on.

Inside the box: a Firestone High School sweatshirt, heather gray with the falcon logo cracked and peeling across the chest. A rubber wristband, the kind they handed out at assemblies, faded from whatever bright color it used to be to a soft, pale gray. And a Polaroid.

I pick up the Polaroid first. The colors have shifted — everything's gone warm, amber-tinted, like the memory itself is blushing. Two girls on a rooftop. One with red hair caught mid-laugh, flying across her face in a blur. One in a denim jacket, arm slung around the other girl's shoulders, grinning at the camera like she's daring it to look away.

The girl in the denim jacket is me. I barely recognize her. She looks — free isn't the word. She looks like she hasn't been told yet. Like she doesn't know what's coming. Like she still believes she gets to choose.

I set the photo down and pick up the wristband. The rubber is soft between my fingers, softer than it should be after all these years, worn down by — what? A decade of being touched by no one? Or the months before, when I wore it every day, when I slept in it, when I twisted it around my wrist during chemistry class until the teacher told me to stop fidgeting?
The memory comes like a door opening. Not slowly. All at once.

Akron. May. Senior year. The Firestone roof — the one you could access through the maintenance door that was always broken, the one everyone knew about and no one fixed because the vice principal's kid was the first one to climb up there. We're sitting with our legs hanging over the edge, not because it's dangerous but because the ledge is the only place that's flat enough to sit on, and below us the parking lot is empty because it's seven p.m. on a Thursday and everyone else is at the prom pre-party.

Jacqueline isn't going to prom. I know this because she told me, casually, like she was telling me the weather. "Not my scene," she said, and I knew what that meant because I'd learned to read Jacqueline the way I'd later learn to read Tom — not by what she said but by what she didn't. What she didn't say: tickets are forty dollars and her mother's between jobs again and she's been wearing the same two pairs of jeans since October and she'd rather miss prom than let anyone see the math of her life.

I'm going to prom. Not because I want to but because Brett Hargrove asked me — Tom's younger cousin, if you can believe it, and I didn't believe it then, the way the Hargroves have been orbiting my life like a slow gravity, pulling me in before I knew I was moving. But that's later. Right now it's the roof, and the sky is doing that Ohio thing where it can't decide if it wants to rain or not, and Jacqueline reaches into her jacket pocket and pulls out the wristband.
"Here," she says. Not offering. Placing it in my hand. Closing my fingers around it.
"What is this?"
"It's a wristband, Viv. Keep up."
"I know it's a wristband. I mean—"
"Wear it." She looks at me. Jacqueline has this way of looking at you where you feel like you're the only person in the world who's ever been seen. "Wear it to your prom. So you don't forget me."

"How could I forget you?" I say it like it's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard. Like forgetting Jacqueline Vance is in the same category as forgetting my own name, or forgetting how to breathe.

Jacqueline smiles. Not the big grin from the Polaroid — smaller, tighter, the one she saves for things that are true and tender and a little bit painful. "People forget," she says. "It's what they do."
"I won't."
"Everyone says that."
"I'm not everyone."
She looks at me for a long time. The sky opens up and it starts to rain, just a little, just enough to make the rooftop dark and slick, and neither of us moves. "Okay," she says finally. "Prove it."

I sit on the closet floor and I hold the wristband and I think about proving it. I think about twelve years. I think about the prom, where I wore the wristband under my corsage, where I touched it during every slow dance like a talisman, like a promise. I think about the morning after, when I took it off to shower and set it on the bathroom counter and when I came back Brett— Tom, I mean Tom— had moved it. "What's with the ugly rubber thing?" he said, and I didn't answer, and I found it later in the trash and I fished it out and I put it in the box and I never wore it again.
I never wore it again.
God. The things we throw away because someone else tells us they're ugly. The things we let go because it's easier than fighting for them. The wristband. The rooftop. Jacqueline. Me.

I pull the sweatshirt over my head. The heather-gray fabric catches on my earrings — the pearl studs Tom's mother gave me — and I yank them out, both of them, and I don't put them back. The sweatshirt doesn't fit. I'm not that girl anymore — I'm longer and leaner, shaped by personal trainers and professional eyebrows and a hundred little adjustments designed to smooth the Akron out of me. The sleeves hit above my wrists. The hem rides up. The falcon logo is cracked beyond recognition.
But I wear it anyway.

I sit on the closet floor with my back against the shoe wall — the shoe wall, Jesus Christ, who has a shoe wall — and I pull my knees to my chest and I feel the cold of the marble floor seeping through the sweatshirt, through the years, all the way to the girl in the denim jacket who looks at the camera like she's daring it to look away.
And I make the decision.

Not because Jax asked. She didn't ask, not really — she offered, she opened a door, but she didn't push me through it. Jax, who has every reason to push, who has every reason to want me on her side like a piece on a board, gave me the space to choose. Which is the most Jacqueline thing she could do and the most terrifying, because it means it has to be my choice. Mine. Not Tom's, not the stylist's, not the campaign consultant's, not anyone's.

And not because the FBI is closing in. Although they are. I'm not stupid — I've seen the phones Tom thinks he's hidden, the calls he takes in the garden, the way he says "everything's fine" in the exact tone that means nothing is. I know the walls are closing in on this life, on this house, on the gilded cage I've been decorating for a decade.

No. I make the decision because I am thirty-two years old and I have never once in my adult life chosen for myself. Not the dresses. Not the house. Not the city. Not the life. Not the man. Not a single goddamn thing since I was eighteen years old and Brett Hargrove's cousin showed up at my college graduation and smiled at me with his whole face and told me I was "exactly the kind of girl who could go places," and I believed him because I was twenty-two and I didn't know yet that "go places" meant "go where I tell you."

I pull out my phone. My real phone. The one on the family plan, the one Tom could check the bill for, the one that says — in every digit and every data point and every call log entry — I am not hiding anymore.

I dial her number. I know it by heart. I've known it by heart since Akron, since the roof, since the night she wrote it on my arm in Sharpie because I'd forgotten my phone and she said, "You're not getting out of this that easy."
She picks up on the second ring.
"Viv?" Low. Careful. Like she's holding something fragile and she knows it.
"I'm in." My voice doesn't shake. I didn't know it wouldn't shake until I say it, until the words are out and they're solid and they're mine. "Whatever the plan is. I'm in."
A pause. I can hear her breathing — or maybe that's me, or maybe it's both of us, or maybe it's the sound of a decade of silence finally breaking.
"Are you sure?" Jax's voice is steady, but there's a current under it. She's giving me every door. Every exit. She's not pulling me through. "There's no halfway in this."

I close my eyes. The closet smells like cedar and old rubber and rain in Akron. The floor is cold against my legs. The sweatshirt doesn't fit but I'm wearing it and I can feel the falcon logo against my chest like a heartbeat.
"I've been halfway my whole life," I say. "I'm done with halfway."

Another pause. Longer this time. And then Jax says, "Okay," and the way she says it — rough, resolved, a little bit broken, a lot bit ready — I can hear her smiling. That tight, tender smile from the rooftop. The one she saves for things that are true.
"Okay," I say back.

We hang up. The silence after is enormous. It fills the closet like water, like air, like the space between the girl I was and the woman I'm becoming, and for the first time in twelve years, the silence doesn't scare me. It sounds like possibility. It sounds like a door I'm finally walking through instead of around.

I stand up. My legs are stiff from the floor, from sitting like a teenager hiding from her mother, which — let's be honest — is exactly what I was doing. I smooth down the sweatshirt. It doesn't help. It still doesn't fit. I still look ridiculous — pearl earrings gone, hair uncombed, wearing a cracked Firestone High School sweatshirt with designer leggings and Tom's least favorite pair of flats.
I don't care.
I walk out of the closet.

The hallway is long and white and gallery-lit, same as always, but it looks different now. Shorter, maybe. Less like a museum and more like a hallway — just a hallway, just a space between rooms, just a thing I can walk through and leave behind.

Tom is coming out of the study. His phone is in his hand and his jaw is doing that thing and he isn't looking up — he's reading something on the screen, something that's making the vein in his forehead pulse, and then he sees me.
He stops.

He looks at me the way you'd look at a stranger who walked into your house and started moving the furniture. Like I'm an intruder. Like I'm a problem he didn't anticipate. Like I'm something that doesn't belong in his gallery home with his curated wife and his controlled, careful life.
"What are you wearing?"

Three words. That's all it takes. Three words that contain twelve years — every time he asked me to change, every time he told me that dress was "too much" or "not enough" or "not appropriate," every time he looked at me like I was a project he was managing instead of a person he was married to. What are you wearing. As if the answer could ever matter to anyone but me. As if my clothes have ever been anything but his to approve.
I don't answer.

I walk past him. Close enough to smell his cologne — the one I bought him two Christmases ago, the one his assistant reminded me about because I'd forgotten, the one that smells like sandalwood and obligation. He doesn't touch me. He doesn't reach out. He just stands there in his white hallway with his phone in his hand and his mouth half open, watching me go.
I don't look back.
For the first time in twelve years, Vivienne Sterling is wearing something she chose. And she's about to choose everything else.
